summary: A solid sound‑board transfer (with a bit of tape hiss) that captures an electrifying 1972 European show – highlighted by a monster Dark Star > Morning Dew and a transcendent Two Souls in Communion.
review: The recording, sourced from the original soundboard and lightly remastered, is clear enough to enjoy despite some residual tape hiss. The performance itself is widely praised for its intensity, especially the 30‑minute Dark Star leading into an epic Morning Dew, plus standout renditions of Two Souls in Communion, Hey Bo Diddley and several rare Pigpen‑centric numbers.

ai_rating.rationale: Most reviewers give five stars and highlight exceptional musical moments; recording quality is good with only minor hiss, yielding a combined high rating.

recording_quality.technical_notes: Remastered soundboard transfer; tape hiss noticeable at the start but fades quickly, overall clarity is strong and instruments (organ, guitar) are well‑defined.

standout_songs: Sitting On Top of the World
Goin' Down the Road Feelin' Bad
poor_songs: Dark Star (mixed note from one reviewer)
show_quality.setlist_flow: The first set is laid‑back but solid, featuring classic renditions of Mr. Charlie and Pigpen’s Two Souls. The second set launches into a sprawling Dark Star that, while divisive for one listener, builds into an intense Morning Dew before moving through high‑energy numbers like Hey Bo Diddley and closing on a tight Not Fade Away.

band_member_comments.Jerry: Praised for soaring guitar tone, organ work on Good Lovin', sustained leads in Dark Star and strong bends alongside Phil's bass.
band_member_comments.Phil: Noted for rumbling bass foundations during the Dark Star jam.
band_member_comments.Bob: Weir’s rhythm guitar/vocals highlighted in early Dark Star passages; his vocal performance on Jack Straw called “brilliant”.
band_member_comments.Keys: Keith’s keyboard work praised on Chinatown Shuffle; Pigpen’s organ and piano contributions (Two Souls, Good Lovin') receive special mention.
band_member_comments.Drums: Bill Kreutzmann’s toms described as “devil‑sliding” in the Dark Star intro; drum segment within the DS noted for its power.
